items.title: Welcome to Non-Deterministic Ramblings
items.text: Welcome to this web site, where I hope to be able to record some slivers of wisdom drawn from many years working in IT. Along the way I have met with people and circumstances, many nice but occasionally some not so pleasant, but from each hopefully I have learned something, and if I record it here I hope that you too may find something of value, if only for any humour.

items.title: "Non-Deterministic"
items.text:
A long, long time ago I studied Computer Science, and while I wasn't very good at the wider mathematics courses, I felt a keen affinity with Discrete Mathemmatics. Part of the discrete maths course concerned itself with Non-Deterministic Finite Automata, or NDFA. By all means follow the links for more information, but in those relatively early days, we studied what is now Automata Theory as a precursor to understanding syntax analysis.

For many years, I joked that for all the time we spent studying NDFA I never had one cross my desk, and I think the probable cause which really defines a lot of my reflections in this blog, are that at the time I studied, we did not have the resources to actually build the range of tools we have now that do use state graphs within them.

More recently I have studied Systems Thinking as a post-graduate abd as part of systems modelling we very much get into Determinism. This is when I think of NDFA and smile to myself, as an NDFA as a state machine could not determine an absolutes state given the input. And so it is with my thinking, in producing this blog my ideas seem to go off in myriad directions, rarely taking teh same path twice from any given starting point.

So please excuse the randomness of the pasts, hopefully they do follow in some sort of logical progression

items.title: "Ramblings"
items.text:
Roughly, I have tried to divide the posts on this into under three broad sections

  • Reflecting - We came, we saw, we experienced, and we would like to help you not to fall into the same traps as maybe we did
  • Thinking - in an area dominated by formal certifications and methods, but latterly a paucity of research finding, we develop heuristics that we think may help explain some of the things we see happening in real-world situations
  • Doing - some words about how I have gone about doing some of the IT things I do

I hope that there is something here that will amuse, maybe promote thoughts of your own, and if it does help in any way then my work here is done.

items.title: Russell
items.text:
Despite attempts using AI to make me look like some sort of secret agent, my career has spoanned well over 40 years. Starting at a time when it was called Cpmputing, I have worked in Inofrmation Technology aka "IT", battled with the Nexus of Forces, and emrged blinking into the light of Digitalisation. EVen now as I write this during summer 2026, the emergence of AI is changing things once again.

Over that time, I started as a progranmnmer, then moved to Product Management, before spending the last 25 years as variously a Solution Architect, an Enterprise Architect, and latterly focussing on Business Architecture

Academnically I have a BSc in Computer Science, an MBA, and an MSC in Systems Thinking in Practice.

Hopefully this site contains aspects drawn from a wide variety of experiences. At the top of this site you'll find liinks to ways to contact me.

items.title: WordPress
items.text: I have been endeavouring to create this web site for several years. When doing my MSc, I did create a WordPress site which exists to this day, but it was something that (a) I kept playing with themes of (never being satisfied with a theme), and (b) I struggled t= that every time I returned to create a post that WordPress had changed the writing tools.
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Quarto
items.text:
For various reasons, I ended up using R to do the statistical analysis required for my MSc thesis. THis took me to the Posit tools, notably RStudio.

I was aware of the ability to use RMarkdown as a publishing tool, but cognitively getting my brain around using and publishing made it a step too far at a time when I was trying to learn so many things at the same time

And then Quarto happened... if you haven't seen QUarto from Posit, then it is a well engineered and superbly supported solution, that fits seamlessly into RStudio and so felt very familiar and easy to use (but then RMarkdown was already engineered into RStudio, and so I kink myself that I probably missed a trick there)

So why isn't this site created using Quarto?

Good question! The answer is two fold:

  • Firstly, while the Quarto documentation is excellent as showing how to publish on Posit's own free web hosting, it felt very off the beaten track internet wise.
  • Secondly, Quarto had simply too many options, for me it was back to that cognitive overload, and I seemed to spending way too much time trying to make options that should have worked, work, only to find that under Option A, facility X was not enabled.
  • Thirdly, RStudio takes a bit of getting used to as a serious writing tool. Quarto is very flexible, and the extensive support covers ways to use Quarto withe other IDEs.
  • Finally (this is getting very Monty Python), trying to publish Quarto to GitHub Pages (where you are now) looked quite difficult compared to Posit's won hosting service.

Let me be clear, for serious publishing work, I'd persevere with Quarto as it is just so powerful. Maybe another blog post?
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Zensical
items.text:
And so we arrive at this site, created using Zensical. I heard the lead developer, Martin Donath talking about his work on a podcast. Zensical is a major update to a HTML documentation tool.

So yes, I am still climbing my way up a steep learning curve, but I got in the ground floor when, compared to Quarto, Zensical is relatively simple. And more importantly, the publishing to GitHub Pages is pretty well documented, hence my arrivel here.

It hasn't been plain sailing, again maybe another blog post
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: What Have Learned Along the Way
items.text: That this stuff takes a lot of work
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Architecting in the organisational culture
items.text:
Does Organisational Culture Impact the delivery of Architecture

We can complete our training in architecture, and pass our certifications. But on going back to our organisations and producing architrectural artafects as we learned, do they have any real impact as part of devlivring our value as archietcts?

items.title: Frameworks vs Examples
items.text:
How Effectieve is is setting Archicturral Frameworks

Most architectural approaches advocate the setting or Architectue Frameworks to direct and giovern the delivery of architecture. Given the potential gap between examples given at training, and the real needs of any organisation, is there a better way to establish architectural standards?

items.title: What is the purpose of an Architect?
items.text:
HWhy can't Architects Agree on what an Architect does?

Get a grouo of architects together and finding consensus on what an architect does, based on our expeirneces arising from our practice, tends to prove very difficult to do. Why si this, and what impact could that have on training
path: What is the purpose of an Architect?
